name: astro-sedpy

metadata.name: astro-sedpy
metadata.summary: Simple tools for astronomical spectral energy distributions, particularly filter projections.

documentation:
# sedpy

Modules for storing and operating on astronomical source spectral energy distributions.

```{image} https://github.com/bd-j/sedpy/workflows/Tests/badge.svg
:target: https://github.com/bd-j/sedpy/actions?query=workflow%3ATests
```

```{image} https://readthedocs.org/projects/sedpy/badge/?version=latest
:alt: Documentation Status
:target: https://sedpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/?badge=latest
```

## Installation & setup:

`sedpy` is pip installable:

```shell
python -m pip install astro-sedpy
```

Or you can install the latest version from github:

```shell
git clone https://github.com/bd-j/sedpy
cd sedpy
pip install .
```

Then in python, e.g.,

```python
from sedpy import observate
# get magnitude from a spectrum:
filt = observate.Filter("sdss_r0")
mag = filt.ab_mag(angstroms, f_lambda_cgs)
# or get several magnitudes at once
filterlist = observate.load_filters(["galex_NUV", "sdss_r0"])
mags = observate.getSED(angstroms, f_lambda_cgs, filterlist=filters)
```

For the filters available by default see the [filter_list].
For adding transmission curves, see these [docs].

This code can be referenced as:

```{image} https://zenodo.org/badge/DOI/10.5281/zenodo.4582723.svg
:target: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.4582723
```

## Description:

- `observate` has methods for generating synthetic photometry through any filters,
  and classes for dealing with filters generally. There is some functionality for spectra
  (vaccum to air conversions).
  With a huge debt to Mike Blanton's [kcorrect](https://github.com/blanton144/kcorrect) code .
- `attenuation` contains simple dust attenuation methods.
- `smoothing` methods for smoothing well-sampled spectra.
- `extinction` (Deprecated) classes for a detailed modeling of extinction curves,
  following the Fitzpatrick & Massa parameterizations.
  See [dust_extinction](https://dust-extinction.readthedocs.io/en/stable/) instead.

[docs]: docs/transmissions.rst
[filter_list]: sedpy/data/filters/README.md

modules.name: sedpy.reference_spectra
documentation:
reference_spectra.py  - load and store Vega and Solar reference spectra.

Units of the reference spectra are AA and erg/s/cm^2/AA
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: sedpy.boneyard.image.run_command
documentation: Open a child process, and return its exit status and stdout

functions.name: sedpy.boneyard.image.read_sexcat
documentation:
Read a SExtractor results catalog, and return a numpy record array.
Because astropy Tables are annoying.

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.image.Image.sextract
documentation:
Run SExtractor on the image.  One should already have a default.param
and a default.sex file laying around with most of the options set.  Howewver, any of the
options can be set by supplying them as keywords, using a similar format
to the commandline options.  Note that errors may be raised if the number  of
apertures does not match the number specified in default.params.

N.B command line changing of phot_apertures NOT WORKING

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.image.Image.point_source_phot
documentation:
Do simple aperture photomtry at a list of locations.  The list
is ideally the record array returned by read_sexcat.

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.image.Image.area_phot
documentation:
Add up all the flux in some (large) regions of the sky defined
by region objects. Fractional pixels not considered, so make
your polygon big (or magnify the image and WCS).

modules.name: sedpy.boneyard.plotsed
documentation: This code is an unfinished attempt to plot SEDs as probability
distributions p(flux, wave)

classes.name: sedpy.boneyard.plotsed.Bunch
documentation: Simple storage.

modules.name: sedpy.boneyard.photometer
documentation:
photometr.py - Simple Aperture photometry.  Very old code, superceded by
astropy affiliated package `photutils.`
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: sedpy.boneyard.photometer.circle_frac_quick
documentation:
obtain fractional pixel coverage.  optionally use subpixels to
increase precison (though this doesn't seem to help).  Assumes pixel
centers have coordinates X.5, Y.5
--- Pixnum methods ---

functions.name: sedpy.boneyard.photometer.quartile_sky
documentation:
Use the median and 16th percentile to estimate the standard
deviation per pixel.
--- SKY statistics determination methods ---

functions.name: sedpy.boneyard.photometer.gaussfit_sky
documentation:
Fit a gaussian to the lower part of a histogram of the sky values.
The histogram bins are estimated using Bayesian blocks.  p_thresh gives
the percentile below which the gaussian is fitted to the data. Return
central value and estimate of standard deviation per pixel

functions.name: sedpy.boneyard.photometer.gmm_sky
documentation:
Use a gaussian mixture model, via expectation maximization.
of course, there's only one gaussian.  could add another for
faint sources, bad pixels, but...

functions.name: sedpy.boneyard.photometer.sigclip_sky
documentation: Use iterative sigma clipping

functions.name: sedpy.boneyard.photometer.centroid
documentation:
Dumb dumb centroiding.  assumes x and y axes are the
last two dimensions of images.  Something is wrong with the
broadcasting.  absolutely *have* to include weighting
--- Centroiding ---

classes.name: sedpy.boneyard.photometer.Photometer

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.photometer.Photometer.measure_flux
documentation:
Measure background subtracted flux.  Takes an aperture object,
and a local background object.

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.photometer.Photometer.object_flux
documentation:
Measure total flux (source + background) within an aperture.
Takes an image, and an aperture object.

Trying for a better class dependence.  Basically wraps the image
in an object with photometry methods.  Incomplete

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.photometer.Aperture.object_flux
documentation: Measure total flux within an aperture (source + background)

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.photometer.Aperture.measure_flux
documentation: Measure background subtracted flux.

classes.name: sedpy.boneyard.photometer.Background
documentation: --- Classes for sky measurement ---

classes.name: sedpy.boneyard.photometer.ZeroSky
documentation:
A class for sky values of zero, or for user defined sky statistics.
The return_value is a tuple giving (sky, sky_area, sigma_sky_per_pixel)

modules.name: sedpy.boneyard.modelgrid
documentation:
modelgrid.py  - Deprecated code for interpolating regular or irregular grids
of models.  Use `scipy.interpolate.RegularGridInterpolator` or
`scipy.interpolate.LinearNDInterpolator`.

Kept here since it is used by some other (old) packages of mine.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: sedpy.boneyard.modelgrid.ModelLibrary

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.modelgrid.ModelLibrary.structure_array
documentation:
Turn a numpy array of floats into a structurd array.

```
param values

ndarray, shape(nrec, nfield).
Values of the structure.

param fieldnames

string sequence of length nfield.
A list or string array of parameter names with length
NFIELD.

param types

string sequence, optional
Format identifiers for each field. Defaults to '<f8'.

returns struct

A numpy structured array.
```

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.modelgrid.ModelLibrary.join_struct_arrays
documentation:
From some dudes on StackOverflow. Add equal length
structured arrays to produce a single structure with fields
from both.

```
param arrays

A sequence of structured arrays.  These must each have the
same length.

returns newarray

A single array containing the fields of arrays.
```

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.modelgrid.ModelLibrary.model_weights
documentation:
Given an array of target coordinates and optionally a list
of parnames, return the indices and weights of the model grid
points that will interpolate to the target points.

```
param target_points

ndarray, shape(ntarg, npar)
The points to which you want to interpolate.  Can be a
numpy structured array of length NTARG or a simple numpy
array of dimension [NTARG x NPAR], if parnames is also
passed.  If a numpy structured array, then the field names
should correspond to parameters in the  structure.
1d arrays (i.e. a single target point) will be
automatically upgraded to 2d arrays

param parnames

string sequence
A list of the parameter names corresponding to the NPAR
dimension of target_points.  These names should correspond
to parameters in the  structure.

param subinds

An optional array identifying the members of the grid to
be used for interpolation.

param itype

string (default 'dt')
A string giving the type of interpolation to perform.
Available options are:

'dt' - use Delaunay triangulation.  This is suitable for
irregular grids, though care must be taken that target
points are within the convex hull described by the grid

'idw' - inverse distance weighting to the NPAR nearest
neighbors.

'nearest' - nearest neighbor, as determined by a kd-tree

'ndlinear' - not yet implemented'

param force_triangulation

bool (default True)
Normally the triangulation and kdtree formed from the
model grid is stored and reused unless the
`triangle_dirtiness' attribute is greater than zero.  If
this keyword is true, force the triangulation and kdtree
to be regenerated regardless of 'dirtiness'

source:35: (WARNING/2) Inline interpreted text or phrase reference start-string without end-string.

returns inds

ndarray, shape(ntarg, nind)
An array that indexes the model parameter grid to give the
interpolating models for each target point.  It has shape
(NTARG, NIND) where nind is NPAR+1 for Delaunay
triangulation, NPAR for 'idw', and 1 for 'nearest'

returns weights

ndarray, shape(ntarg, nind)
The interpolation weight for each model grid point
specified by inds, and of identical shape. The weights
summed along the NIND direction will always be 1. Thus for
'nearest' weights is always 1.
```

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.modelgrid.ModelLibrary.refresh_graphs
documentation:
Given parameter names and optionally specific model
indices, build a a Delaunay triangulation and a KDTree for the
model points.

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.modelgrid.ModelLibrary.weightsDT
documentation:
The interpolation weights are determined from barycenter
coordinates of the vertices of the enclosing Delaunay
triangulation simplex. This allows for the use of irregular Nd
grids. See also weights_1DLinear and
weights_kNN_inverse_distance.

```
param target_points

ndarray, shape(ntarg,npar)
The coordinates to which you wish to interpolate.

returns inds

ndarray, shape(ntarg,npar+1)
The model indices of the interpolates.

returns weights

narray, shape (ntarg,npar+1)
The weights of each model given by ind in the
interpolates.
```

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.modelgrid.ModelLibrary.weights_kNN_inverse_dist
documentation:
The interpolation weights are determined from the inverse
distance to the k nearest neighbors.

```
param target_points

ndarray, shape(ntarg,npar)
The coordinates to which you wish to interpolate.

param k

The number of nearest neighbors to use.

returns inds

ndarray, shape(ntarg,npar+1)
The model indices of the interpolates.

returns weights

narray, shape (ntarg,npar+1)
The weights of each model given by ind in the
interpolates.
```

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.modelgrid.ModelLibrary.weights_1DLinear
documentation:
The interpolation weights are determined from 1D linear
interpolation.

```
param model_points

ndarray, shape(nmod)
The parameter coordinate of the available models

param target_points

ndarray, shape(ntarg)
The coordinate to which you wish to interpolate

returns inds

ndarray, shape(ntarg,2)
The model indices of the interpolates

returns weights

narray, shape (ntarg,2)
The weights of each model given by ind in the interpolates.
```

Class to deal with (irregular) grids of models.  Primary
attribute is `pars`: a structured array of parameter values of
shape (ngrid).  Methods are provided to manipulate the parameter
structure, and to obtain interpolation weights for specific points
within the grid using a variety of methods.

classes.name: sedpy.boneyard.modelgrid.SpecLibrary

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.modelgrid.SpecLibrary.spectra_from_pars
documentation:
This should take a numpy structured array of parameters of
length nobj and return an (nobj, nwave) array of spectra

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.modelgrid.SpecLibrary.generateSEDs
documentation:
:returns sed:
    ndarray of shape (nobj,nfilter)

:returns lbol:
    ndarray of shape (nobj)

:returns outspectra:
    ndarray of shape (nobj,nwave)

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.modelgrid.SpecLibrary.interpolate_to_pars
documentation:
Method to obtain the model spectrum for a given set of
parameter values via interpolation of the model grid. The
interpolation weights are determined from barycenters of a
Delaunay triangulation or nLinear interpolation or
k-nearest-neighbor inverse distance.

```
param target_points

ndarray, shape (ntarg,ndim)
Desired model parameters. Can also be a structured array
with fields named for the model parameters.  See
ModelLibrary.model_weights()

param subinds

ndarray
Indices of the model pars structure to use in
interpolation. Allows for only portions of the model
library to be used.

param parnames

string sequence, len (ndim)
The names of the model library parameters

returns spectra

ndarray, shape (ntarg, nwave)
The interpolated spectra.
```

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.modelgrid.SpecLibrary.combine_weighted_spectra
documentation:
Weight self.spectra using broadcasting, then sum the
weighted spectra. Should add a switch to sum the weights
first so that the output is a single spectrum.

```
param inds

shape (ntarg,nint)
Indices of the models to sum

param weights

shape (ntarg, nint)
Weights of the models corresponding to inds.

returns spec

shape (nwave,ntarg).
The weighted sum.
```

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.modelgrid.SpecLibrary.read_model_from_fitsbinary
documentation: Read spectra from a fits binary table in a certain format.

Class to operate on spectral libraries. Methods are provided to
interpolate the available model spectra (stored as a structured
parameter array and a spectral array) to a certain set of
parameters.  Subclasses are used to return the actual model
spectrum given a set of model parameters.  Primary attributes are
pars, spectra, wavelength.  Spectra should be of shape (NOBJ x
NWAVE)

modules.name: sedpy.boneyard.ds9region
documentation: -- VERY PRELIMINARY!!!! -----

functions.name: sedpy.boneyard.ds9region.get_image_angle
documentation:
:returns angle:
   In radians, degrees East of North

functions.name: sedpy.boneyard.ds9region.world2pix
documentation: Wrap the wcs world2pix method to be less finicky and take our defaults.

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.ds9region.Ellipse.contains
documentation:
Determine whether pixel locations x,y are within the ellipse.
Requires that a WCS be given.  Assumes tangent projection, and
will fail for large ellipses/images. Assumes N is up and E to
the left.

x, and y are zero indexed, and can be produced by flattened versions of
`y, x = np.indices(image)`

methods.name: sedpy.boneyard.ds9region.Polygon.contains
documentation:
Determine whether pixel locations x,y are within the polygon.
Requires that a WCS be given.  Uses Path objects from matlib,
should probably use Shapely, or better yet a real spherical
coordinates implementation

modules.name: sedpy._version
documentation: file generated by setuptools_scm
don't change, don't track in version control

modules.name: sedpy.observate
documentation:
observate.py - Python module for storing filter information and tools for
projecting spectra onto filters.  Also includes tools for convolving spectra.

Assumed input units are erg/s/cm^2/AA and AA
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: sedpy.observate.rebin
documentation:
Rebin (instead of interpolate) transmission onto given wavelength array,
preserving total transmission.  This is useful if the output wavelength
array is more coarseley sampled than the native transmission array, to avoid
interpolating over important sharp features in the transmission function

Stripped down version of specutils.FluxConservingResampler

FIXME: implement as NUFFT?  Do something about the jaggedness (high frequency noise)
introduced when the output is more finely sampled than the input?
Useful utilities

functions.name: sedpy.observate.load_filters
documentation:
Given a list of filter names, this method returns a list of Filter
objects.

Parameters
----------
filternamelist : list of strings
    The names of the filters.

Returns
-------
filterlist : list of Filter() instances
    A list of filter objects.

functions.name: sedpy.observate.getSED
documentation:
Takes wavelength vector, a flux array and list of Filter objects and
returns the SED in AB magnitudes.

# Parameters

sourcewave

: Spectrum wavelength (in Angstroms)

sourceflux

: Associated flux (assumed to be in erg/s/cm^2/AA)

filterlist

: The filters onto which to project the spectra

linear_flux

: If True return maggies, else AB mags

# Returns

sed

: broadband magnitudes or maggies.

functions.name: sedpy.observate.list_available_filters
documentation:
Return a list of filter names that are available by default, i.e. which
have been installed in the sedpy/data/filters/ directory.

functions.name: sedpy.observate.Lbol
documentation:
Calculate the bolometric luminosity of a spectrum or spectra.

Parameters
----------
wave : ndarray
   The wavelength vector of length nwave.

spec : ndarray
   The spectra, of shape (...,nsource, nwave), in F_lambda.

wave_min : float
   Minimum wavelength for the integral.

max_wave : float
   Maximum wavelength for the integral

Returns
-------
lbol :
   The bolometric luminosity, integrated from wave_min to wave_max.  Array
   of length (...nsource)
Routines for spectra

functions.name: sedpy.observate.air2vac
documentation:
Convert from in-air wavelengths to vacuum wavelengths. Based on Allen's
Astrophysical Quantities.

# Parameters

air

: The in-air wavelengths.

# Returns

vac: ndarray of shape `(N_pix,)`

: The corresponding vacuum wavelengths.

functions.name: sedpy.observate.vac2air
documentation:
Convert from vacuum wavelengths to in-air wavelengths. Follows the SDSS
statement of the IAU standard from Morton 1991 ApJS.

vac2air(air2vac(wave)) yields wave to within 1 part in a million over the
optical range.

# Parameters

vac: ndarray of shape `(N_pix,)`

: The vacuum wavelengths.

# Returns

air

: The corresponding in-air wavelengths.

variables.name: sedpy.observate.lightspeed
documentation: AA/s

classes.name: sedpy.observate.Filter

methods.name: sedpy.observate.Filter.__init__
documentation:
Read in the filter data, calculate and cache some properties of the
filters.

methods.name: sedpy.observate.Filter.load_filter
documentation:
Read a filter in simple two column ascii format and populate the
wavelength and transmission arrays.  The first column is wavelength in
AA and the second column is transmission (detector signal per photon)

Parameters
----------
filename : string
    The fully qualified path and filename of the file that contains the
    filter transmission.

methods.name: sedpy.observate.Filter._process_filter_data
documentation:
Clean up transmission data and assign to attributes.

# Parameters

wave

: Wavelength, in Angstroms, of the transmission curve

trans

: Filter transmission at the wavelengths corresponding to `wave`

methods.name: sedpy.observate.Filter._remove_extra_zeros
documentation:
Remove extra leading or trailing zero transmission points.  This
leaves one zero before (after) the first (last) non-zero transmission
point, if they were present in the original transmission function.

Parameters
----------
min_trans : float (optional, default: 1e-5)
    Defines zero, in terms of fraction of the maximum transmission.

methods.name: sedpy.observate.Filter.gridify_transmission
documentation:
Place the transmission function on a regular grid in lnlam
(angstroms) defined by a lam_min and dlnlam.  Note that only the
non-zero values of the transmission on this grid stored.  The indices
corresponding to these values are stored as the `inds` attribute (a
slice object). (with possibly a zero at either end.)

Parameters
----------
dlnlam : float
    The spacing in ln-lambda of the regular wavelength grid onto which
    the filter is to be placed.

wmin : float (optional, default: 100)
    The starting wavelength (Angstroms) for the regular grid.

methods.name: sedpy.observate.Filter.get_properties
documentation:
Determine and store a number of properties of the filter and store
them in the object.  These properties include several 'effective'
wavelength definitions and several width definitions, as well as the
in-band absolute AB solar magnitude, the Vega and AB reference
zero-point detector signal, and the conversion between AB and Vega
magnitudes.

See Fukugita et al. (1996) AJ 111, 1748 for discussion and definition
of many of these quantities.

methods.name: sedpy.observate.Filter.ab_to_vega
documentation:
The conversion from AB to Vega systems for this filter. It has the
sense

$m_{Vega} = m_{AB} + Filter().ab_to_vega$

methods.name: sedpy.observate.Filter.display
documentation: Plot the filter transmission curve

methods.name: sedpy.observate.Filter.obj_counts_hires
documentation:
Project source spectrum onto filter and return the detector signal.

# Parameters

sourcewave

: Spectrum wavelength (in Angstroms). Must be monotonic increasing.

sourceflux

: Associated flux (assumed to be in erg/s/cm^2/AA)

# Returns

counts

: Detector signal(s).

methods.name: sedpy.observate.Filter.obj_counts_lores
documentation:
Project source spectrum onto filter and return the detector
signal. This method differs from `obj_counts_hires` in that the
source spectrum is interpolated onto the transmission spectrum instead
of vice-versa, which is necessary with the source spectrum does not
adequately sample features in the transmission spectrum.

# Parameters

sourcewave

: Spectrum wavelength (in Angstroms). Must be monotonic increasing.

sourceflux

: Associated flux (assumed to be in erg/s/cm^2/AA)

# Returns

counts

: Detector signal(s).

methods.name: sedpy.observate.Filter.obj_counts_grid
documentation:
Project source spectrum onto filter and return the detector
signal. This method differs from `obj_counts_*res` in that the source
spectrum is assumed to be interpolated onto a logarithmic grid in
lambda with spacing and minimum wavelength given by `dlnlam` and `wmin`
filter attributes.

# Parameters

sourceflux

: Source flux (assumed to be in erg/s/cm^2/AA) assumed to be on the
  logarithmic grid defined by `wmin` and `dlnlam`, with wavelength
  ascending.

source_offset

: The (hypothetical) element of the sourceflux array corresponding to
  observed frame wavelength of `wmin`. Should be negative if the first
  element of sourcewave corresponds to a wavelength > wmin.

# Returns

counts

: Detector signal(s).

methods.name: sedpy.observate.Filter.obj_counts
documentation:
Project a spectrum onto a filter and return the detector signal.
This method uses the keywords `lores` and `gridded` to choose between
the various projection algorithms.

# Parameters

sourcewave

: Spectrum wavelength (in Angstroms). Must be monotonic increasing.

sourceflux

: Associated flux (assumed to be in erg/s/cm^2/AA)

lores

: Switch to interpolate the source spectrum onto the wavelength grid
  of the filter transmission curve, instead of the vice-versa (which
  is the default). Useful for a spectrum with sparse sampling in
  wavelength compared to the filter transmission curve (e.g. narrow
  bands and BaSeL lores spectra)

gridded

: Switch to accomplish the filter projection via simple sums. This
  can be faster, but assumes the sourcewave and sourceflux are on a
  logarithmic wavelength grid given by the dlnlam and wmin attributes
  of the Filter object.

# Returns

counts

: Detector signal(s).

methods.name: sedpy.observate.Filter.ab_mag
documentation:
Project source spectrum onto filter and return the AB magnitude.

# Parameters

sourcewave

: Spectrum wavelength (in Angstroms). Must be monotonic increasing.

sourceflux

: Associated flux (assumed to be in erg/s/cm^2/AA)

# Returns

mag

: AB magnitude of the source(s).

methods.name: sedpy.observate.Filter.vega_mag
documentation:
Project source spectrum onto filter and return the Vega magnitude.

# Parameters

sourcewave

: Spectrum wavelength (in Angstroms). Must be monotonic increasing.

sourceflux

: Associated flux (assumed to be in erg/s/cm^2/AA)

# Returns

mag

: Vega magnitude of the source(s).

class_variables.name: sedpy.observate.Filter.ab_gnu
documentation: AB reference spctrum in erg/s/cm^2/Hz

This class operates on filter transmission files. It reads simple
2-column ascii files containing filter transmission curves. It caches a
number of useful filter quantities. Methods are provided to project a
source spectrum onto the filter and return the magnitude, AB or Vega.

# Parameters

kname

: The kcorrect style name of the filter, excluing '.par',
  e.g. sdss_r0.

nick

: A nickname to associate with the filter.

directory

: The path to the directory containing the filter file. If not given
  then the sedpy/data/filters/ directory will be searched.

dlnlam

: If given, interpolate the transmission curve onto a grid in
  $\ln(\lambda)$ with this spacing (and begining at `wmin`)

wmin

: If `dlnlam` is supplied then `wmin` gives the minimum wavelength
  (in Angstroms) for the grid.

min_trans

: The minimum transmission value (as fraction of the maximum transmission
  value) to consider as a useful positive value. Useful for curves that
  have extremely low amplitude components far from the main part of the
  filter when speed is more important than accuracy.

data: None or tuple of ndarray (optional, default None)

: If provided, a 2-tuple of ndarrays giving wavelength (AA) and
  transmission (detector signal per photon).

classes.name: sedpy.observate.FilterSet

methods.name: sedpy.observate.FilterSet.__init__
documentation:
Parameters
----------
filterlist : list of strings
    The filters to include in this set, in order

wmin : float
    Minimum wavelength (AA) for the wavelength grid for the FilterSet

dlnlam : float
    logarithmic spacing for the wavelength grid of the FilterSet

methods.name: sedpy.observate.FilterSet._set_filters
documentation:
Set the filters and the wavelength grid

native : list of Filter() instances
    The Filter objects that will be part of this FilterSet, including
    their native transmission

methods.name: sedpy.observate.FilterSet._build_super_trans
documentation:
Build the global gridded transmission array. Populates FilterSet.trans, which
is an array of shape (nfilters, nlam) giving $T   imes \lambda    imes
d\lambda / Z$ where `T` is the detector signal per photon and `Z` is the
detector signal for a 1 maggie source

methods.name: sedpy.observate.FilterSet.interp_source
documentation:
Interpolate spectra onto the global wavelength grid.

# Parameters

inwave

: Input wavelength (Angstroms)

sourceflux

: Input source fluxes at the wavelengths given by `inwave`

# Returns

flux

: Interpolated flux. Note the transpose relative to input if `N_source > 1`

methods.name: sedpy.observate.FilterSet.get_sed_maggies
documentation:
Compute the flux in maggies of the source through the given filters.

# Parameters

sourceflux

: Source flux (assumed to be in erg/s/cm^2/AA)

sourcewave: ndarray (optional)

: If supplied, the wavelength in Angstroms. Otherwise it is assumed
  to be on the same wavelength grid as FilterSet.lam, saving the cost
  of an interpolation.

# Returns

maggies

: The flux (in maggies) of the sources through the filters.

methods.name: sedpy.observate.FilterSet.get_sed_maggies_trapz
documentation:
Use vectorized trapz to project filters.  This can be
slower than the default get_sed_maggies

A class representing a set of filters, and enabling fast projections of
sources onto these filters via pre-computation, caching, and a global
wavelength array.

modules.name: sedpy.extinction
documentation:
extinction.py  - Classes for implementing detailed dust extinction models.

Old code, use with caution!
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

classes.name: sedpy.extinction.Attenuator

methods.name: sedpy.extinction.Attenuator.attenuate_spectrum
documentation: This will be slow until the dust curves are vectorized.

methods.name: sedpy.extinction.Attenuator.draw_taus
documentation: Not implemented

A level of abstraction around the dust curves, which allows for
some generic methods. The Attenuator is composed of an (effective)
DustLaw and a DustDistribution class, both of which can be easily
extended.  The first takes wavelength and tau_v as arguments and
returns     au_\lambda. The second takes arguments that can be
anything (e.g. stellar age, metallicity) and returns a
distribution of tau_V

classes.name: sedpy.extinction.GenericCurve

methods.name: sedpy.extinction.GenericCurve.fm_curve
documentation:
Fitzpatrick & Massa parameterization of the UV excess
curve.

methods.name: sedpy.extinction.GenericCurve.drude
documentation:
Drude profile for the 2175AA bump.

```
param x

Inverse wavelength (inverse microns) at which
values for the drude profile are requested.

param gamma

Width of the Drude profile (inverse microns).

param x0

Center of the Drude profile (inverse microns).

returns k_lambda

The value of the Drude profile at x
```

methods.name: sedpy.extinction.GenericCurve.f_90
documentation:
UV rise in the excess curve, from FM90 (defined cubic
polynomial).

```
param x

Inverse wavelength in inverse microns

returns

Cubic polynomial at x centered on 5.9 microns**(-1), as in
FM90
```

methods.name: sedpy.extinction.GenericCurve.f_07
documentation:
UV rise in the excess curve, from FM07 (quadratic with
variable center).

```
param x

Inverse wavelength in inverse microns.

param c5

Pivot point for the quadratic.

returns

Quadratic at x centered on c5, as in FM07.
```

methods.name: sedpy.extinction.GenericCurve.powerlaw
documentation:
Power-law shape of the color excess curve, for the NIR
extinction curve, as in FM09.

```
param x

Inverse wavelength in inverse microns.

param R_v

(default: 3.1)
Slope of the optical portion of the curve.

param k

(default: None)
Power-law normalization.

param alpha

(default: -1.84)
Power-law slope.
```

methods.name: sedpy.extinction.GenericCurve.spline
documentation:
Cubic spline, for the optical portion of the extinction
curve.

```
param x

Inverse wavelength in inverse microns.

param spline_x

Location of the spline anchor points, in inverse microns.

param spline_k

Value at the spline anchor points.
```

methods.name: sedpy.extinction.GenericCurve.acurve
documentation: Return the extinction curve given the excess curve.

Class for handling extinction curves with the parameterization
of Fitzpatrick & Massa 1990 or F&M 2007
Extinction Law Classes

classes.name: sedpy.extinction.FM07

methods.name: sedpy.extinction.FM07.default_pars
documentation:
FM07 parameterization coefficients, based on MW stars.

```
param var

Set to True to include scatter in the coefficients
based on the FM07 sample.
```

methods.name: sedpy.extinction.FM07.selfupdate
documentation:
Enforce empirical relationships between some of the
exctinction curve parameters, optionally including variance in
the relationships.  These are based on data in FM09 and Gordon
et al 2002.

methods.name: sedpy.extinction.FM07.ecurve
documentation:
Excess curve, including 3 components (UV, Optical, and
NIR).
Extinction curves from Fitzpatrick and Massa 2007

methods.name: sedpy.extinction.LMC.default_pars
documentation:
Gordon 2003 parameterization coefficients.  Set var to True
to include scatter in the coefficients based on the Gordon 03
sample.

methods.name: sedpy.extinction.SMC.default_pars
documentation:
Gordon 2003 parameterization coefficients.  Set var to True
to include scatter in the coefficients based on the Gordon 03
sample.

classes.name: sedpy.extinction.F99

methods.name: sedpy.extinction.F99.default_pars
documentation:
FM99 parameterization coefficients, for an R_v dependent
curve.  Set var to True to include scatter in the coefficients
based on the FM07 sample.

methods.name: sedpy.extinction.F99.selfupdate
documentation:
Enforce empirical relationships between some of the
exctinction curve parameters.

methods.name: sedpy.extinction.F99.ecurve
documentation: Excess curve, including 2 components (UV and Optical/NIR)

Fitzpatrick 1999 R_v dependent extinction curves.  These are a
one parameter family, though in practice we allow the bump
strength to vary.

modules.name: sedpy.attenuation
documentation:
attenuation.py  - A collection of simple functions implementing various
proposed attenuation curves.
-*- coding: utf-8 -*-

functions.name: sedpy.attenuation.powerlaw
documentation:
Simple power-law attenuation, normalized to 5500AA.

```
param wave

The wavelengths at which optical depth estimates are desired.

param tau_v

(default: 1)
The optical depth at 5500AA, used to normalize the
attenuation curve.

returns tau

The optical depth at each wavelength.
```

functions.name: sedpy.attenuation.calzetti
documentation:
Calzetti et al. 2000 starburst attenuation curve, with extrapolations to
the FUV and NIR.

```
param wave

The wavelengths at which optical depth estimates are desired.

param tau_v

(default: 1)
The optical depth at 5500AA, used to normalize the attenuation curve.

param R_v

(default: 4.05)
The ratio of total selective extinction, parameterizing the slope of
the attenuation curve.  A_v = R_v * E(B-V)

returns tau

The optical depth at each wavelength.
```

functions.name: sedpy.attenuation.drude
documentation:
Drude profile for the 2175AA bump.

```
param x

Inverse wavelength (inverse microns) at which values for the drude
profile are requested.

param gamma

Width of the Drude profile (inverse microns).

param x0

Center of the Drude profile (inverse microns).

returns k_lambda

The value of the Drude profile at x, normalized such that the peak is 1.
```

functions.name: sedpy.attenuation.noll
documentation:
Noll 2009 attenuation curve. This is based on the Calzetti curve, with
added variable bump (as Drude) and overall slope change. Any extra
keywords are passed to the Drude (e.g. x0, gamma, both in inverse microns).

```
param wave

The wavelengths at which optical depth estimates are desired.

param tau_v

(default: 1)
The optical depth at 5500AA, used to normalize the attenuation curve.

param Ebump

(default: 0.0)
Stength of the 2175AA bump.  Normalizes the Drude profile.

param delta

(default 0.)
Slope of the power-law that modifies the Calzetti curve.

param c_r

Constant used to alter R_v=A_V/E(B-V) of the curve.  To maintain the
Calzetti R_v=4.05, use c_r = -delta.  Note that even with c_r = -delta
the calzetti curve will not be recovered unless delta=0

returns tau

The optical depth at each wavelength.
```

functions.name: sedpy.attenuation.chevallard
documentation:
au_v dependent attenuation curves matched to disk RT models,
as in Chevallard et al. 2013. No UV bump (or indeed tests in the
UV at all).

```
param wave

The wavelengths at which optical depth estimates are desired.

param tau_v

(default: 1)
The optical depth at 5500AA, used to normalize the
attenuation curve.

returns tau

The optical depth at each wavelength.
```

functions.name: sedpy.attenuation.conroy
documentation:
Conroy & Schiminovich 2010 dust attenuation curves including a
decreased UV bump.

```
param wave

The wavelengths at which optical depth estimates are desired.

param tau_v

(default: 1)
The optical depth at 5500AA, used to normalize the
attenuation curve.

param R_v

(default: 3.1)
The ratio of total selective extinction, parameterizing the
slope of the attenuation curve.  A_v = R_v * E(B-V)

param f_bump

(default: 0.6)
The strength of the 2175AA UV bump, as a fraction of the bump
strength in Cardelli et al. extinction curve.

returns tau

The optical depth at each wavelength.
```

functions.name: sedpy.attenuation.broken_powerlaw
documentation:
Attenuation curve as in V. Wild et al. 2011, i.e. power-law
slope can change between regions. Superceded by Chevallard 2013
for optical/NIR.

```
param wave

The wavelengths at which optical depth estimates are desired.

param tau_v

(default: 1)
The optical depth at 5500AA, used to normalize the
attenuation curve.

returns tau

The optical depth at each wavelength.
```

functions.name: sedpy.attenuation.wg00
documentation:
Witt+Gordon 2000 DIRTY radiative transfer results, for
idealized geometries.

functions.name: sedpy.attenuation.cardelli
documentation:
Cardelli, Clayton, and Mathis 1998 Milky Way extinction curve,
with an update in the near-UV from O'Donnell 1994

```
param wave

The wavelengths at which optical depth estimates are desired.

param tau_v

(default: 1)
The optical depth at 5500AA, used to normalize the
attenuation curve.

param R_v

(default: 3.1)
The ratio of total selective extinction, parameterizing the
slope of the attenuation curve.  A_v = R_v * E(B-V)

returns tau

The optical depth at each wavelength.
```
EXTINCTION CURVES

functions.name: sedpy.attenuation.smc
documentation:
Pei 1992 SMC extinction curve.

```
param wave

The wavelengths at which optical depth estimates are desired.

param tau_v

(default: 1)
The optical depth at 5500AA, used to normalize the
attenuation curve.

returns tau

The optical depth at each wavelength.
```

functions.name: sedpy.attenuation.lmc
documentation:
Pei 1992 LMC extinction curve.

```
param wave

The wavelengths at which optical depth estimates are desired.

param tau_v

(default: 1)
The optical depth at 5500AA, used to normalize the
attenuation curve.

returns tau

The optical depth at each wavelength.
```

modules.name: sedpy.smoothing
documentation: smoothing.py  - Spectral smoothing functionality

functions.name: sedpy.smoothing.smoothspec
documentation:
# Parameters

wave

: The wavelength vector of the input spectrum. Assumed Angstroms.

spec

: The flux vector of the input spectrum.

resolution

: The smoothing parameter. Units depend on `smoothtype`.

outwave

: The output wavelength vector. If `None` then the input wavelength
  vector will be assumed, though if `min_wave_smooth` or
  `max_wave_smooth` are also specified, then the output spectrum may
  have different length than `spec` or `wave`, or the convolution may
  be strange outside of `min_wave_smooth` and `max_wave_smooth`.
  Basically, always set `outwave` to be safe.

smoothtype

: The type of smoothing to perform. One of:

  - `"vel"` - velocity smoothing, `resolution` units are in km/s
    (dispersion not FWHM)
  - `"R"` - resolution smoothing, `resolution` is in units of
    $\lambda/ \sigma_\lambda$ (where $\sigma_\lambda$ is
    dispersion, not FWHM)
  - `"lambda"` - wavelength smoothing. `resolution` is in units of
    Angstroms
  - `"lsf"` - line-spread function. Use an aribitrary line spread
    function, which can be given as a vector the same length as `wave`
    that gives the dispersion (in AA) at each wavelength. Alternatively,
    if `resolution` is `None` then a line-spread function must be
    present as an additional `lsf` keyword. In this case all additional
    keywords as well as the `wave` vector will be passed to this `lsf`
    function.

fftsmooth

: Switch to use FFTs to do the smoothing, usually resulting in massive
  speedups of all algorithms. However, edge effects may be present.

min_wave_smooth

: The minimum wavelength of the input vector to consider when smoothing
  the spectrum. If `None` then it is determined from the output
  wavelength vector and padded by some multiple of the desired resolution.

max_wave_smooth

: The maximum wavelength of the input vector to consider when smoothing
  the spectrum. If None then it is determined from the output wavelength
  vector and padded by some multiple of the desired resolution.

inres

: If given, this parameter specifies the resolution of the input. This
  resolution is subtracted in quadrature from the target output resolution
  before the kernel is formed.

  In certain cases this can be used to properly switch from resolution
  that is constant in velocity to one that is constant in wavelength,
  taking into account the wavelength dependence of the input resolution
  when defined in terms of lambda. This is possible iff:
  \* `fftsmooth` is False
  \* `smoothtype` is `"lambda"`
  \* The optional `in_vel` parameter is supplied and True.

  The units of `inres` should be the same as the units of
  `resolution`, except in the case of switching from velocity to
  wavelength resolution, in which case the units of `inres` should be
  in units of lambda/sigma_lambda.

in_vel

: If supplied and True, the `inres` parameter is assumed to be in units
  of lambda/sigma_lambda. This parameter is ignored **unless** the
  `smoothtype` is `"lambda"` and `fftsmooth` is False.

# Returns

flux

: The smoothed spectrum on the `outwave` grid, ndarray.

functions.name: sedpy.smoothing.smooth_vel
documentation:
Smooth a spectrum in velocity space. This is insanely slow, but general
and correct.

# Parameters

wave

: Wavelength vector of the input spectrum.

spec

: Flux vector of the input spectrum.

outwave

: Desired output wavelength vector.

sigma

: Desired velocity resolution (km/s), dispersion *not* FWHM.

nsigma

: Number of sigma away from the output wavelength to consider in the
  integral. If less than zero, all wavelengths are used. Setting this
  to some positive number decreses the scaling constant in the O(N_out *
  N_in) algorithm used here.

inres

: The velocity resolution of the input spectrum (km/s), *not* FWHM. the
  spectrum will be smoothed by the quadrature difference between the
  desired resolution and `inres`

# Returns

smoothed_spec

: The smoothed spectrum

functions.name: sedpy.smoothing.smooth_vel_fft
documentation:
Smooth a spectrum in velocity space, using FFTs. This is fast, but makes
some assumptions about the form of the input spectrum and can have some
issues at the ends of the spectrum depending on how it is padded.

# Parameters

wavelength

: Wavelength vector of the input spectrum.

spectrum

: Flux vector of the input spectrum.

outwave

: Desired output wavelength vector.

sigma_out

: Desired velocity resolution (km/s), dispersion *not* FWHM

inres

: The velocity resolution of the input spectrum (km/s), *not* FWHM. the
  spectrum will be smoothed by the quadrature difference between the
  desired resolution and `inres`

# Returns

smoothed_spec

: The smoothed spectrum

functions.name: sedpy.smoothing.smooth_wave
documentation:
Smooth a spectrum in wavelength space. This is insanely slow, but
general and correct (except for the treatment of the input resolution if it
is velocity)

# Parameters

wave

: Wavelength vector of the input spectrum.

spec

: Flux vector of the input spectrum.

outwave

: Desired output wavelength vector.

sigma

: Desired resolution (dispersion *not* FWHM) in wavelength units. This
  can be a vector of same length as `wave`, in which case a wavelength
  dependent broadening is calculated

nsigma

: Number of sigma away from the output wavelength to consider in the
  integral. If less than zero, all wavelengths are used. Setting this to
  some positive number decreses the scaling constant in the O(N_out *
  N_in) algorithm used here.

inres

: Resolution of the input, in either wavelength units or
  $\lambda/d\lambda \cdot (c/v)$. The spectrum will be smoothed by
  the quadrature difference between the desired resolution and `inres`

in_vel

: If True, the input spectrum has been smoothed in velocity
  space, and `inres` is assumed to be in lambda/dlambda.

# Returns

smoothed_spec

: The smoothed spectrum.

functions.name: sedpy.smoothing.smooth_wave_fft
documentation:
Smooth a spectrum in wavelength space, using FFTs. This is fast, but
makes some assumptions about the input spectrum, and can have some
issues at the ends of the spectrum depending on how it is padded.

# Parameters

wavelength

: Wavelength vector of the input spectrum.

spectrum

: Flux vector of the input spectrum.

outwave

: Desired output wavelength vector.

sigma

: Desired resolution (dispersion *not* FWHM) in wavelength units.

inres

: Resolution of the input, in wavelength units (dispersion not FWHM). The
  spectrum will be smoothed by the quadrature difference between the
  desired resolution and `inres`

# Returns

smoothed_spec

: The smoothed spectrum.

functions.name: sedpy.smoothing.smooth_lsf
documentation:
Broaden a spectrum using a wavelength dependent line spread function.
This function is only approximate because it doesn't actually do the
integration over pixels, so for sparsely sampled points you'll have
problems. This function needs to be checked and possibly rewritten.

# Parameters

wavelength

: Wavelength vector of the input spectrum.

spectrum

: Flux vector of the input spectrum.

outwave

: Desired output wavelength vector.

sigma

: The dispersion (not FWHM) as a function of wavelength that you want to
  apply to the input spectrum. `None` or ndarray of same length as
  `outwave`. If `None` then the wavelength dependent dispersion will
  be calculated from the function supplied with the `lsf` keyward.

lsf

: A function that returns the Gaussian dispersion at each wavelength.
  This is assumed to be in sigma, not FWHM.

return_kernel

: If True, return the kernel used to broaden the spectrum as ndarray of
  shape `(nout, nin)`.

# Extra Parameters

lsf_kwargs

: Passed to the function supplied in the `lsf` keyword.

# Returns

smoothed_spec

: The smoothed spectrum.

functions.name: sedpy.smoothing.smooth_lsf_fft
documentation:
Smooth a spectrum by a wavelength dependent line-spread function, using
FFTs.

# Parameters

wavelength

: Wavelength vector of the input spectrum.

spectrum

: Flux vector of the input spectrum.

outwave

: Desired output wavelength vector.

sigma

: Dispersion (in same units as `wave`) as a function `wave`. If not
  given, sigma will be computed from the function provided by the `lsf`
  keyword.

lsf

: Function used to calculate the dispersion as a function of wavelength.
  Must be able to take as an argument the `wave` vector and any extra
  keyword arguments and return the dispersion (in the same units as the
  input wavelength vector) at every value of `wave`. If not provided
  then `sigma` must be specified.

pix_per_sigma

: Number of pixels per sigma of the smoothed spectrum to use in
  intermediate interpolation and FFT steps. Increasing this number will
  increase the accuracy of the output (to a point), and the run-time, by
  preserving all high-frequency information in the input spectrum.

preserve_all_input_frequencies

: This is a switch to use a very dense sampling of the input spectrum that
  preserves all input frequencies. It can significantly increase the call
  time for often modest gains...

eps

: Deprecated.

# Extra Parameters

kwargs:

: All additional keywords are passed to the function supplied to the
  `lsf` keyword, if present.

# Returns

smoothed_spec

: The smoothed spectrum.

functions.name: sedpy.smoothing.smooth_fft
documentation:
Basic math for FFT convolution with a gaussian kernel.

# Parameters

dx

: The wavelength or velocity spacing, same units as `sigma`

spec

: The spectrum flux vector

sigma

: The width of the Gaussian kernel, same units as `dx`

# Returns

smoothed_spec

: The smoothed spectrum, on the same grid as the input.

functions.name: sedpy.smoothing.mask_wave
documentation:
Restrict wavelength range (for speed) but include some padding based on
the desired resolution.

functions.name: sedpy.smoothing.resample_wave
documentation:
Resample spectrum, so that the number of elements is the next highest
power of two.  This uses np.interp.  Note that if the input wavelength grid
did not critically sample the spectrum then there is no gaurantee the
output wavelength grid will.

functions.name: sedpy.smoothing.subtract_input_resolution
documentation:
Subtract the input resolution (in quadrature) from a target output
resolution to get the width of the kernel that will convolve the input to
the output.  Assumes all convolutions are with gaussians.
